title: Everyone pays the price as patent holders on seeds stifle innovation
gist: A small number of seed companies control 70-94 percent of US row-crop seed markets and use patents to suppress competition and capture farm subsidies, but a May 2026 DOJ Antitrust Division court filing in Corteva v. Inari signals the government may be turning against the extension of these patent rights.

insights.claim: Two companies control more than 70 percent of US corn and soybean seed sales, and the top four cottonseed companies control nearly 94 percent of that market, per USDA.
insights.speaker: The Conversation
insights.so_what: Extreme concentration defines pricing power in agricultural inputs and is the target of emerging antitrust scrutiny.
insights.locator: The US Department of Agriculture has found

insights.claim: Genetically engineered seed prices have risen 463 percent since 1990 while prices farmers receive for crops rose only 56 percent over the same period.
insights.speaker: The Conversation
insights.so_what: Quantifies the margin transfer from farmers to seed companies, evidence of monopoly pricing power.
insights.locator: According to a report from the Department of Agriculture's Economic Research Service

insights.claim: An August 2025 study found that for every 1 percent increase in farm subsidies, seed companies raise their prices by 0.5 percent, capturing public money intended for farmers.
insights.speaker: The Conversation
insights.so_what: Demonstrates a mechanism by which taxpayer subsidies are arbitraged into corporate profit, strengthening the antitrust case.

insights.claim: Farmers are price takers whose input and equipment costs can be manipulated by consolidated suppliers, per Iowa farmer Noah Coppess's October 2025 Senate Judiciary testimony.
insights.speaker: The Conversation
insights.so_what: Political testimony adds momentum to legislative and regulatory action on seed and fertilizer competition.
insights.quote: we're price takers rather than price makers
insights.locator: Testifying at an October 2025 Senate Judiciary Committee hearing

insights.claim: Seed companies use patents and restrictive licensing plus infringement-lawsuit threats to block university and independent researchers from sequencing or breeding with protected seeds.
insights.speaker: The Conversation
insights.so_what: Suppressed independent research is the innovation-stifling harm at the center of the regulatory challenge.

insights.claim: Because no one outside the dominant companies (not even the US government) knows which patented crops are vulnerable to emerging pests and pathogens, the food supply carries an unassessed genetic-monoculture risk.
insights.speaker: The Conversation
insights.so_what: Frames seed-patent secrecy as a systemic food-security vulnerability, a tail risk for agricultural and food markets.
insights.locator: The result is that no one outside of the dominant companies

insights.claim: A May 2026 DOJ court filing in Corteva's 2023 patent suit against startup Inari argued companies should not be able to restrict the public from sequencing genetic material deposited as part of patent protection.
insights.speaker: The Conversation
insights.so_what: A favorable ruling could open seed genetics to competitors and check seed prices, a potential inflection point for agbiotech incumbents.
insights.locator: In 2023, multinational agrochemical company Corteva sued

insights.claim: The filing came from the DOJ Antitrust Division rather than the Civil Division that usually handles IP, signaling the government views the patent extension as an illegitimate way to exclude competitors.
insights.speaker: The Conversation
insights.so_what: The choice of division is a tell that antitrust enforcers are taking aim at seed-patent practices, raising regulatory risk for Corteva and peers.
insights.locator: Notably, the department's court filing came from the Antitrust Division
